structured.summary: This paper addresses the tradeoff between in-distribution (ID) and out-of-distribution (OOD) performance in finetuning pretrained language models for natural language generation. It proposes two methods—ensembling full and lightweight finetuned models, and cocktail finetuning via distillation from a lightweight model to a full finetuned model—to achieve strong performance both ID and OOD, supported by empirical results and theoretical analysis.

strengths.point: Identifies and addresses a significant tradeoff in finetuning pretrained language models between ID and OOD performance.
strengths.evidence: Section 1 Introduction and Section 5 Results of Full and Lightweight Finetuning describe the tradeoff and quantify it across multiple datasets.

strengths.point: Proposes simple yet effective methods—ensembling and cocktail finetuning—that achieve the best of both ID and OOD performance.
strengths.evidence: Section 6 Combining Models with a Weighted Ensemble and Section 7 Cocktail Finetuning present the methods and show empirical improvements in Table 2.

strengths.point: Provides a theoretical explanation for why distillation on ID data can transfer OOD behavior in a multiclass logistic regression setting with large output spaces.
strengths.evidence: Section 9 Why Does Distillation on ID Data Improve OOD? includes propositions and proofs explaining the phenomenon.

strengths.point: Evaluates methods on multiple natural language generation tasks and datasets, including summarization, table-to-text, and open-domain QA.
strengths.evidence: Section 4 Experiments Setup details datasets and tasks; results are reported in Section 6 and 7.

weaknesses.point: The ensemble method, while effective, doubles inference time and memory usage, which may limit practical deployment.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 6.3 Runtime and Memory discusses the increased computational cost of ensembling.

weaknesses.point: Cocktail finetuning shows slightly worse OOD performance compared to ensembling, indicating imperfect transfer of OOD behavior.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 7.2 Results notes a slight decay in OOD performance relative to ensembles.

weaknesses.point: The approach requires tuning the mixture weight λ on in-distribution validation data, which may not always correlate perfectly with OOD performance.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 6.1 Choosing the Mixture Weight λ explains selection based on ID validation data without OOD data.

weaknesses.point: Limited exploration of lightweight finetuning methods beyond adapters and prefix-tuning; other methods might behave differently.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 1 and 4 focus on adapters and prefix-tuning as representative lightweight finetuning methods.

weaknesses.point: The theoretical analysis is limited to a simplified multiclass logistic regression setting, which may not fully capture complexities of large pretrained language models.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 9 provides theory in a logistic regression framework, which abstracts away from deep model architectures.

weaknesses.point: Some hyperparameter tuning for open-domain QA used OOD validation data, which is not realistic in practice.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.2 notes use of OOD data for limited hyperparameter tuning in open-domain QA experiments.

weaknesses.point: The paper does not explore or report on the impact of these methods on generation quality metrics beyond ROUGE and exact match, such as human evaluation or diversity.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.1 and results focus on ROUGE-2 and exact match metrics without mention of human or qualitative evaluation.

weaknesses.point: The paper lacks discussion on potential limitations or failure cases of the proposed methods.
weaknesses.evidence: No explicit section or discussion on limitations or scenarios where the methods may not work well.

questions.question: How sensitive are the ensemble and cocktail finetuning methods to the choice of the mixture weight λ, especially when ID validation data is limited or not fully representative?
questions.motivation: Understanding robustness to λ selection is important for practical deployment where validation data may be scarce or not perfectly representative.

questions.question: Can the cocktail finetuning approach be extended or adapted to incorporate unlabeled OOD data to further improve OOD performance?
questions.motivation: Incorporating unlabeled OOD data could potentially enhance robustness and address the slight OOD performance gap compared to ensembling.

questions.question: Have the authors considered or tested the applicability of their methods to other lightweight finetuning approaches such as BitFit or LoRA?
questions.motivation: Evaluating broader applicability would strengthen claims about the generality of the proposed methods.

questions.question: Could the authors provide insights or hypotheses on why distillation from a lightweight finetuned model transfers OOD behavior effectively despite training only on ID data?
questions.motivation: A deeper understanding of this phenomenon could guide future method development and theoretical work.

critique_points.point_001: The ensemble method, while effective, doubles inference time and memory usage, which may limit practical deployment. (Section 6.3 Runtime and Memory discusses the increased computational cost of ensembling.)
critique_points.point_002: Cocktail finetuning shows slightly worse OOD performance compared to ensembling, indicating imperfect transfer of OOD behavior. (Section 7.2 Results notes a slight decay in OOD performance relative to ensembles.)
critique_points.point_003: The approach requires tuning the mixture weight λ on in-distribution validation data, which may not always correlate perfectly with OOD performance. (Section 6.1 Choosing the Mixture Weight λ explains selection based on ID validation data without OOD data.)
critique_points.point_004: Limited exploration of lightweight finetuning methods beyond adapters and prefix-tuning; other methods might behave differently. (Section 1 and 4 focus on adapters and prefix-tuning as representative lightweight finetuning methods.)
critique_points.point_005: The theoretical analysis is limited to a simplified multiclass logistic regression setting, which may not fully capture complexities of large pretrained language models. (Section 9 provides theory in a logistic regression framework, which abstracts away from deep model architectures.)
critique_points.point_006: Some hyperparameter tuning for open-domain QA used OOD validation data, which is not realistic in practice. (Section 4.2 notes use of OOD data for limited hyperparameter tuning in open-domain QA experiments.)
critique_points.point_007: The paper does not explore or report on the impact of these methods on generation quality metrics beyond ROUGE and exact match, such as human evaluation or diversity. (Section 4.1 and results focus on ROUGE-2 and exact match metrics without mention of human or qualitative evaluation.)
critique_points.point_008: The paper lacks discussion on potential limitations or failure cases of the proposed methods. (No explicit section or discussion on limitations or scenarios where the methods may not work well.)
critique_points.point_009: How sensitive are the ensemble and cocktail finetuning methods to the choice of the mixture weight λ, especially when ID validation data is limited or not fully representative? (Understanding robustness to λ selection is important for practical deployment where validation data may be scarce or not perfectly representative.)
critique_points.point_010: Can the cocktail finetuning approach be extended or adapted to incorporate unlabeled OOD data to further improve OOD performance? (Incorporating unlabeled OOD data could potentially enhance robustness and address the slight OOD performance gap compared to ensembling.)
critique_points.point_011: Have the authors considered or tested the applicability of their methods to other lightweight finetuning approaches such as BitFit or LoRA? (Evaluating broader applicability would strengthen claims about the generality of the proposed methods.)
critique_points.point_012: Could the authors provide insights or hypotheses on why distillation from a lightweight finetuned model transfers OOD behavior effectively despite training only on ID data? (A deeper understanding of this phenomenon could guide future method development and theoretical work.)
